refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR conflates three distinct functional obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies implementation choice) and focuses on the core problem: making worksite geometry available to downstream planning and execution functions. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design iteration.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that a worksite spatial model exists in a defined format (e.g., point cloud, mesh, or feature set) and can be queried by planning/execution functions to retrieve feature geometry and attributes within specified accuracy tolerances.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface geometry data from the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and classify static obstacles (e.g., structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent work-zone boundaries and exclusion zones per operational policy.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Integrate terrain, obstacle, and work-zone data into a unified spatial model accessible to planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The parent FR (CR-NGHE-006) remains composite; the sub-FRs are atomic and ready for independent DP and IM assignment.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign zone-level feature inventories (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; maintain as hierarchical zone model with zone-to-feature mappings; provide zone-based query API.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a voxel grid or occupancy map with per-cell attributes (occupancy state, feature type, safety zone); update incrementally as new observations arrive; expose as rasterized spatial query interface.

DP4: Acquire raw sensor streams (camera, lidar, IMU) and defer feature extraction to downstream consumers; store raw data with timestamps and sensor calibration metadata; provide time-indexed data access without feature interpretation.

DP5: Integrate worksite safety plan and operational policies as constraints on feature representation; encode permitted/forbidden zones, access rules, and hazard buffers directly into the spatial model; expose constrained query interface that respects policy boundaries.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP5 (integrated): Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; represent as a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints); integrate worksite safety plan and operational policies as explicit constraints on feature accessibility and hazard buffers; expose via constraint-aware query interface for planning/execution consumers.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor fusion logic and feature classification could reduce modularity if classification rules are hardcoded into fusion pipeline; mitigate by separating fusion (DP1 core) from classification (IM boundary).
Policy constraint encoding (DP5) could couple spatial model to specific safety rule syntax; mitigate by using declarative constraint language independent of policy domain.
Coordinate frame unification assumes known sensor extrinsics; if calibration is unstable or multi-robot, frame consistency becomes a hidden dependency; document calibration assumptions explicitly.
Query interface design must avoid exposing internal graph structure; use abstraction layer to decouple consumer logic from representation details.
coupling_check.notes: DP1+DP5 combination is well-decoupled if: (1) fusion produces a canonical spatial representation (graph/mesh) independent of downstream use cases, (2) policy constraints are applied as a separate interpretation layer, (3) query API is stable and representation-agnostic. Risk: if policies are baked into feature geometry, coupling increases.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensor data is available and can be synchronized to a common timestamp; RISK: sensor latency skew or dropout could degrade fusion quality; MITIGATION: define acceptable synchronization tolerance and fallback to single-modality if fusion fails.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan is static or changes infrequently during a mission; RISK: dynamic policy updates (e.g., new hazard zone declared mid-operation) require model refresh; MITIGATION: design constraint layer to support incremental policy updates without full model rebuild.

ASSUMPTION: Feature classification taxonomy is well-defined and stable; RISK: novel or ambiguous features (e.g., partially obscured obstacles) may not map cleanly to taxonomy; MITIGATION: include 'unknown' or 'uncertain' feature classes and confidence scores.

ASSUMPTION: Spatial model accuracy is sufficient for planning/execution; RISK: if model error exceeds planning tolerance, downstream functions may fail; MITIGATION: quantify model uncertainty (e.g., per-feature confidence, localization error bounds) and expose to consumers.

ASSUMPTION: Query interface latency is acceptable for real-time planning; RISK: large spatial models or complex constraint queries could introduce unacceptable delays; MITIGATION: profile query performance and implement spatial indexing (e.g., octree, R-tree) if needed.

ASSUMPTION: Coordinate frame remains stable across mission duration; RISK: drift in localization or sensor calibration could cause model-to-reality misalignment; MITIGATION: implement periodic re-calibration checkpoints and drift detection.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) required spatial model accuracy and uncertainty quantification (e.g., ±0.1m localization error, per-feature confidence scores), (2) acceptable query latency for planning/execution loops, (3) policy constraint update frequency and mechanism (static vs. dynamic), (4) feature taxonomy and classification confidence thresholds. This will enable tighter DP evaluation and IM boundary definition. Additionally, clarify whether the spatial model must support multi-robot coordination or single-robot operation, as this affects coordinate frame and synchronization assumptions.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for acquiring multi-modal spatial feature data, fusing sensor inputs into a unified coordinate frame, maintaining a structured spatial graph with feature attributes (geometry, classification, safety constraints), integrating worksite safety plans and operational policies as explicit constraints, and exposing a constraint-aware query interface for planning and execution consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound multi-modal sensor streams (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GNSS) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; raw spatial observations and feature detections.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound constraint-aware spatial queries and feature accessibility metadata to planning/execution consumers (Vehicle autonomy, Teleoperation operator interface); includes hazard buffers, safety zones, and policy-enforced feature constraints.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety constraint violations, hazard detections, and policy breaches detected during spatial graph maintenance and query evaluation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional communication for distributed spatial graph synchronization if NGHE Solution or remote planning systems require real-time access to unified spatial representation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural owner of constraint representation, safety plan integration, and hazard buffer logic. Multi-modal sensor fusion and spatial graph maintenance are core safety-critical functions. Constraint-aware query interface aligns with safety policy enforcement. Minor diffusion: raw sensor acquisition remains in Vehicle/Teleoperation; planning consumers (Vehicle autonomy, Teleoperation UI) remain external but receive constraint-enriched queries.

Moderate fit if NGHE is a high-level orchestration/planning layer. Spatial graph and constraint logic would be contained, but sensor fusion and real-time safety constraint enforcement would diffuse to Vehicle and Safety Services. Overly broad; mixes planning orchestration with low-level spatial representation and safety policy enforcement.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/execution platform. Spatial graph maintenance, safety constraint integration, and policy enforcement belong in a centralized, reusable service layer, not embedded in vehicle firmware. High diffusion: safety logic, worksite plans, and query interface would leak into Vehicle control stack and become difficult to update or share across multiple vehicles.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-machine interface and remote control subsystem. Spatial graph, sensor fusion, and safety constraint logic are orthogonal to operator interaction. High diffusion: safety policies and spatial representation would be duplicated or tightly coupled to UI state, complicating maintenance and multi-consumer access.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the optimal module boundary because: (1) it is the designated owner of safety constraints, policies, and hazard management; (2) spatial graph with safety attributes is a safety-critical data structure that must be centralized and versioned; (3) constraint-aware query interface is a safety-enforcement mechanism that belongs in a dedicated service; (4) multi-modal sensor fusion for safety-relevant features is a core safety function; (5) worksite safety plan integration is inherently a Safety Services responsibility. The module exhibits high cohesion (all functions relate to spatial safety representation and constraint enforcement) and low coupling (external consumers query via a well-defined interface; raw sensor streams are ingested from Vehicle/Teleoperation; alerts are published via SafetyAlert).
containment_risks: Real-time latency: Spatial graph updates and constraint queries must meet vehicle autonomy and teleoperation responsiveness requirements. If Safety Services is remote or centralized, network latency on Telemetry and Tasking interfaces could violate safety-critical timing constraints. Mitigation: co-locate Safety Services with Vehicle or implement edge caching.

Sensor fusion complexity: Multi-modal sensor fusion (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GNSS) is algorithmically complex and may require tight coupling to vehicle kinematics and calibration. If Safety Services is decoupled from Vehicle, sensor preprocessing and registration must be well-defined. Mitigation: define clear sensor preprocessing contracts; Safety Services consumes pre-registered, calibrated feature streams.

Scalability of spatial graph: As worksite complexity grows, spatial graph size and query latency may degrade. Distributed spatial indexing or hierarchical representation may be needed. Mitigation: design spatial graph with scalability in mind (e.g., octree, grid-based partitioning); plan for horizontal scaling if needed.

Policy update synchronization: Worksite safety plans and operational policies must be synchronized across Safety Services and all consumers (Vehicle, Teleoperation). Stale or inconsistent policies could lead to safety violations. Mitigation: implement versioning and atomic policy updates; use SafetyAlert to notify consumers of policy changes.

Diffusion of sensor acquisition: Raw multi-modal sensor streams originate in Vehicle and Teleoperation; Safety Services depends on timely, accurate sensor data. If sensor preprocessing is weak, Safety Services cannot guarantee constraint correctness. Mitigation: establish clear sensor interface contracts and validation rules.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The selected DP (DP1 + DP5 integrated) is well-suited to Safety Services containment. However, to reduce latency and coupling risks, recommend clarifying the DP to explicitly separate concerns: (1) DP1a: Acquire and preprocess multi-modal sensor data (owned by Vehicle/Teleoperation, exposed via Telemetry interface); (2) DP1b: Fuse preprocessed sensor streams into unified coordinate frame and maintain spatial graph (owned by Safety Services); (3) DP5: Integrate safety plans and policies as constraints on spatial graph; expose constraint-aware query interface (owned by Safety Services). This decomposition preserves the integrated safety-spatial logic in Safety Services while clarifying sensor acquisition boundaries and reducing coupling to vehicle-specific sensor stacks.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria are concrete: (1) worksite model/map generation is measurable via spatial graph completeness and feature coverage metrics; (2) availability to planning/execution functions is verifiable through interface testing (Tasking interface delivery, latency compliance); (3) safety plan and operational policies integration is auditable via constraint validation against known policy rules. Safety Services IM boundary provides a single, testable point of constraint enforcement.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR maps unambiguously to Safety Services IM: spatial feature capture (Telemetry inbound), unified representation (spatial graph), safety constraint integration (policy engine), and exposure (Tasking outbound). Ownership is explicit; no ambiguity about which module owns spatial graph versioning, constraint evaluation, or policy synchronization.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP (DP1 + DP5 integrated) directly addresses FR. Multi-modal sensing + fusion satisfies 'capture'; structured spatial graph with attributes satisfies 'represent'; constraint integration satisfies 'machine-readable model' with safety semantics. DP is solution-complete and logically sound. No functional gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Sensor acquisition diffusion: Raw multi-modal sensor streams (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GNSS) originate in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. Safety Services depends on timely, calibrated sensor data but does not own sensor hardware or preprocessing. If sensor preprocessing contracts are weak or undefined, Safety Services cannot guarantee spatial graph correctness. Mitigation: establish explicit sensor interface SLAs (calibration accuracy, registration tolerance, latency bounds) and validation rules at Telemetry ingress.

Real-time latency diffusion: Constraint-aware queries (Tasking interface) must meet vehicle autonomy and teleoperation responsiveness requirements (assumed <100ms for safety-critical decisions). If Safety Services is remote or centralized, network latency could violate timing constraints. Mitigation: co-locate Safety Services with Vehicle autonomy stack or implement edge caching with eventual consistency guarantees.

Policy synchronization diffusion: Worksite safety plans and operational policies must remain consistent across Safety Services and all consumers (Vehicle autonomy, Teleoperation UI). Policy updates must be atomic and versioned. If policy distribution is asynchronous or incomplete, consumers may operate under stale constraints. Mitigation: implement versioned policy distribution via SafetyAlert; require consumers to acknowledge policy version before executing tasks.

Spatial graph scalability diffusion: As worksite complexity grows (number of features, sensor density), spatial graph maintenance and query latency may degrade. If Safety Services is not designed for hierarchical or distributed indexing, performance may diffuse to consumers (e.g., Vehicle autonomy experiencing query timeouts). Mitigation: design spatial graph with octree or grid-based partitioning; plan for horizontal scaling or edge replication.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Safety Services is the natural owner of spatial graph, constraint representation, and safety policy enforcement. High cohesion: all functions relate to spatial safety representation and constraint-aware query. Low coupling: external consumers interact via well-defined Tasking interface; raw sensor streams ingested via Telemetry; alerts published via SafetyAlert. Minor diffusion is acceptable and expected: sensor acquisition remains in Vehicle/Teleoperation (appropriate ownership); planning consumers remain external (appropriate separation of concerns). Recommended refinement: decompose DP1 into DP1a (sensor preprocessing, owned by Vehicle/Teleoperation) and DP1b (sensor fusion and spatial graph, owned by Safety Services) to clarify boundaries and reduce coupling to vehicle-specific sensor stacks.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP decomposition: Split DP1 into DP1a (acquire and preprocess multi-modal sensor data; owned by Vehicle/Teleoperation; exposed via Telemetry interface with defined calibration and registration contracts) and DP1b (fuse preprocessed sensor streams into unified coordinate frame and maintain spatial graph; owned by Safety Services). This preserves integrated safety-spatial logic while clarifying sensor acquisition boundaries.

Define sensor interface contracts: Establish explicit SLAs for Telemetry inbound interface: sensor calibration accuracy (e.g., ±5cm for LiDAR), registration tolerance (e.g., ±10cm cross-sensor), latency bounds (e.g., <50ms from sensor to Safety Services), and data validation rules (e.g., outlier detection, consistency checks). Document in interface specification.

Specify latency and availability requirements: Define acceptable latency for Tasking outbound interface (constraint-aware queries) to support vehicle autonomy and teleoperation responsiveness. If <100ms is required, clarify whether Safety Services will be co-located with Vehicle or edge-cached. Document in acceptance criteria.

Implement versioned policy distribution: Design policy update mechanism to ensure atomic, versioned distribution of worksite safety plans and operational policies. Require consumers to acknowledge policy version before executing tasks. Use SafetyAlert to notify consumers of policy changes and version mismatches.

Design spatial graph for scalability: Specify spatial graph data structure (e.g., octree, grid-based partitioning) to support hierarchical queries and horizontal scaling. Plan for edge replication or distributed indexing if worksite complexity grows beyond single-node capacity.

Establish constraint validation testing: Define test cases to verify that Safety Services correctly enforces worksite safety plans and operational policies. Include tests for policy conflicts, constraint violations, and hazard buffer correctness. Automate validation in CI/CD pipeline.

decision.justification: Gate results show FR-DP-IM alignment is sound (all three gates pass with high confidence), but the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that the current DP is logically complete but architecturally under-decomposed. The DP1 (multi-modal sensing) conflates sensor acquisition (owned by Vehicle/Teleoperation) with sensor fusion (owned by Safety Services), creating coupling and diffusion risk. The gate explicitly recommends splitting DP1 into DP1a and DP1b to clarify ownership boundaries. Additionally, four critical sub-concerns emerge from diffusion analysis: (1) sensor interface contracts, (2) real-time latency and co-location strategy, (3) versioned policy distribution, and (4) spatial graph scalability. These are not functional gaps in the current FR (which is well-formed), but rather architectural and operational concerns that must be addressed at the next decomposition level to reduce diffusion risk and ensure safety criticality is met. High safety criticality (risk tailoring factor) mandates explicit treatment of these concerns before implementation. Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition will expose these concerns as distinct functional requirements (e.g., FR1.1: Acquire and preprocess multi-modal sensor data; FR1.2: Fuse preprocessed sensor streams; FR1.3: Enforce versioned policy distribution; FR1.4: Support constraint-aware queries within latency bounds) and allow each to be designed, allocated, and verified independently.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Acquire and preprocess multi-modal sensor data (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GNSS) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems with defined calibration accuracy, registration tolerance, and latency bounds, and expose via Telemetry interface with validation contracts.

FR1.2: Fuse preprocessed multi-modal sensor streams into a unified coordinate frame and maintain a hierarchically-indexed spatial graph with feature attributes, versioning, and scalability support for growing worksite complexity.

FR1.3: Integrate worksite safety plans and operational policies into the spatial graph as machine-readable constraints, and enforce constraint validation with atomic, versioned policy distribution to all consumers via SafetyAlert.

FR1.4: Expose constraint-aware spatial queries via Tasking interface with latency and availability guarantees sufficient to support vehicle autonomy and teleoperation responsiveness (target: <100ms for safety-critical decisions).
FR1.5: Validate spatial graph correctness and constraint enforcement against known safety policies and hazard scenarios, with automated testing and CI/CD integration.

revise.why: DP1 (multi-modal sensing) must be explicitly decomposed into DP1a (sensor acquisition and preprocessing, owned by Vehicle/Teleoperation, exposed via Telemetry interface with calibration and registration contracts) and DP1b (sensor fusion and spatial graph maintenance, owned by Safety Services). This decomposition is recommended by the gate analysis and is necessary to clarify ownership boundaries, reduce coupling to vehicle-specific sensor stacks, and enable independent verification of sensor interface contracts. The revised DP structure will map cleanly to the sub-FRs and reduce diffusion risk.

assumptions: Sensor preprocessing (calibration, registration, outlier detection) is owned by Vehicle/Teleoperation subsystems and exposed via Telemetry interface with explicit SLAs.
Safety Services is co-located with Vehicle autonomy stack or edge-cached to meet <100ms latency requirement for constraint-aware queries.
Worksite safety plans and operational policies are versioned and distributed atomically via SafetyAlert; consumers acknowledge policy version before executing tasks.
Spatial graph is designed with hierarchical indexing (e.g., octree, grid-based partitioning) to support scalability and distributed queries.
Constraint validation is automated and integrated into CI/CD pipeline to ensure safety policies are correctly enforced.
Sensor interface contracts (calibration accuracy, registration tolerance, latency bounds, data validation rules) are formally specified and auditable.
open_risks: Sensor acquisition diffusion: Raw sensor streams originate in Vehicle/Teleoperation; if preprocessing contracts are weak, Safety Services cannot guarantee spatial graph correctness. Mitigation: establish explicit sensor interface SLAs and validation rules at Telemetry ingress.

Real-time latency diffusion: Constraint-aware queries must meet <100ms target; if Safety Services is remote or centralized, network latency could violate timing constraints. Mitigation: co-locate Safety Services with Vehicle autonomy or implement edge caching with eventual consistency.

Policy synchronization diffusion: Worksite safety plans must remain consistent across Safety Services and all consumers; if policy distribution is asynchronous or incomplete, consumers may operate under stale constraints. Mitigation: implement versioned policy distribution via SafetyAlert with consumer acknowledgment.

Spatial graph scalability diffusion: As worksite complexity grows, spatial graph maintenance and query latency may degrade, diffusing to consumers. Mitigation: design spatial graph with hierarchical indexing and plan for horizontal scaling or edge replication.

Safety criticality verification: High safety criticality requires rigorous validation of constraint enforcement, policy conflicts, and hazard buffer correctness. Mitigation: establish comprehensive test cases and automated validation in CI/CD.
evidence_needed: Sensor interface specification: calibration accuracy (e.g., ±5cm for LiDAR), registration tolerance (e.g., ±10cm cross-sensor), latency bounds (e.g., <50ms from sensor to Safety Services), data validation rules.
Latency and availability requirements: acceptable latency for Tasking outbound interface (constraint-aware queries); co-location strategy or edge caching design if <100ms is required.
Policy distribution mechanism: versioned policy update design; consumer acknowledgment protocol; SafetyAlert notification schema.
Spatial graph design: data structure specification (e.g., octree, grid-based partitioning); hierarchical query support; scalability plan for growing worksite complexity.
Constraint validation test cases: policy conflict detection; constraint violation scenarios; hazard buffer correctness; automated CI/CD integration.
Safety criticality assessment: hazard analysis (HARA) or FMEA identifying critical constraints and failure modes; traceability to constraint validation tests.
